coredata.dc:title: Spherical metal mechanism toward revolution of Zn growth for ultrafast plating/stripping kinetics
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Yan-Fei
Jiao, Rui
Shen, Xiao-Yan
Song, Yi-Han
Ding, Lei
Yang, Guo-Duo
Shao, Changlu
Wu, Xing-Long
Zhang, Jing-Ping
Deng, Ming-Xiao
Wang, Shao-Lei
Sun, Hai-Zhu
coredata.dc:description: Aqueous zinc batteries have attracted wide attentions due to their high security and theoretical capacity. However, intrinsic hexagon flake stacking mechanism of Zn metal anode raises uncontrollable Zn dendrite growth and poor electrochemistry property. Herein, innovative spherical Zn metal growth mechanism is firstly proposed assisting by graphene quantum dots (GQDs) through electroplating process. The strongest adsorption energy between Zn2+ and GQDs is formed for preferential GQDs-Zn clusters producing, and then the nucleation of Zn surrounding GQDs is ideally happened. After the subsequent remainder Zn2+ plating, the homogeneous composite Zn spheres are prepared on Cu foam skeleton. Remarkably, GQDs cores fixed in the matrix always lead to the recoverable spherical Zn growth procedure during repetitive plating/stripping processes. Combining the preferential Zn (002) crystal texture, the significant spherical Zn metal anode reaches stable thermodynamics to effective suppression of Zn dendrites growth and side reactions occurrence. Under ultrahigh current density of 20 mA cm−2, the stability of 908 cycles is achieved in half-cell. This work provides a new mechanism for designing neotype Zn metal anode to thoroughly avoid the natural drawbacks of traditional hexagon shape Zn, which is significant for the practice application of aqueous zinc batteries.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Spherical Zn metal
Quantum dots
Crystal texture
Ultrafast kinetics
Aqueous zinc batteries

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Proposition of spherical Zn metal growth mechanism
Sustainability of spherical Zn structure in cycling
Ultrafast plating/stripping kinetics of spherical Zn
Electrochemical tests of aqueous zinc batteries

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 22275030 and 22209023 ), the Fundamental Research Funds (Science and Technology Achievements Transformation) for the Central Universities of China ( CGZH202203 ), the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 22035001 ), and Jilin Provincial Education Department.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM images and (b) FT-IR spectrum of GQDs. XPS test of GQDs for (c) C 1 s and (d) O 1 s. Calculation models and corresponding adsorption energy of Zn ion absorbed on (e) different oxygen-function groups and (f) Cu metal substrate. Schematic illustration of the preparations of (g) Cu@GQDs@Zn and (h) Cu@Zn.

(a-c) SEM images and (d-g) elemental mapping images of Cu@GQDs@Zn-10. (h-i) TEM images of Cu@GQDs@Zn-10 in electroplating process. (j) XRD patterns of Cu@Zn, Cu@GQDs@Zn-5, Cu@GQDs@Zn-10 and Cu@GQDs@Zn-15. Calculation models and corresponding adsorption energy of (k) Zn (002) and (l) Zn (100) absorbed on GQDs cluster structure in different views.

Schematic illustration of Zn stripping/plating processes for (a) Cu@GQDs@Zn-10, (b) Cu@Zn and (c) Zn foil. SEM images of (d-f) Cu@GQDs@Zn-10, (g-i) Cu@Zn, (j-l) Zn foil in (d, g, j) initial state, (e, h, k) Zn stripping, (f, i, l) Zn plating at 1 mA cm−2, 5 mAh cm−2, respectively.

(a) CA curves of Cu@GQDs@Zn-10, Cu@Zn and Zn foil in the symmetric cell at the overpotential of −150 mV. (b) Voltage curves of Cu@GQDs@Zn-10//Cu at various cycles. (c) Comparison of CE stability of Cu@GQDs@Zn-10 in this work with ZnF2@Zn [45], ZnSe@Zn [46], TZNC@Zn [47], Zn||AEC [48], MXene-GF [49], MCM41-Zn [50], N-Zn [51], ZMB@Zn [52], TCNQ@Zn [53], CTF@Zn [54], g-C3N4@Zn [55] and CuZIF-L@TM [56] reported in recent literatures. CE of Cu@GQDs@Zn-10//Cu, Cu@Zn//Cu and Zn foil//Cu at (d) 5 mA cm−2, 1 mAh cm−2 and (e) 20 mA cm−2, 1 mAh cm−2. SEM images of (f) Zn foil, (g) Cu@Zn, (h) Cu@GQDs@Zn-10 and (i, j, k) Cu foil after 200 cycles of CE test at 20 mA cm−2, 1 mAh cm−2.

(a, b) Cycling performance and (c) rate performance in symmetrical cells at (a) 1 mA cm−2, (b) 10 mA cm−2, (c) 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 10 mA cm−2 with the fixed capacity of 1 mAh cm−2 for Cu@GQDs@Zn-10, Cu@Zn and Zn foil. (d, g, j) Digital photographs and (e, f, h, i, k, l) corresponding SEM images for (d-f) Zn foil, (g-i) Cu@Zn and (j-l) Cu@GQDs@Zn-10 after 200 h cycling at 10 mA cm−2 and 1 mAh cm−2. Nyquist plots of (m) Zn foil, (n) Cu@Zn and (o) Cu@GQDs@Zn-10 at the state of before and after 50th cycle.

(a) CV profiles of Cu@GQDs@Zn-10//MnO2, Cu@Zn//MnO2 and Zn foil//MnO2. (b) EIS plots of Cu@GQDs@Zn-10//MnO2 and Cu@Zn//MnO2. (c) Galvanostatic charge/discharge profiles at the first cycle, (d) cycling and (e) rate performance of Cu@GQDs@Zn-10//MnO2, Cu@Zn//MnO2 and Zn foil//MnO2. (f) Cu@GQDs@Zn-10//MnO2 pouch full cell driving LEDs at different states.

head.ce:title: Spherical metal mechanism toward revolution of Zn growth for ultrafast plating/stripping kinetics

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, National & Local United Engineering Laboratory for Power Batteries, Northeast Normal University, No. 5268 Renmin Street, Changchun 130024, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National & Local United Engineering Laboratory for Power Batteries

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Organic Electronics and Information Displays & Institute of Advanced Materials (IAM), School of Chemistry and Life Sciences, Nanjing University of Posts & Telecommunications (NJUPT), Nanjing 210023, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Organic Electronics and Information Displays & Institute of Advanced Materials (IAM)
School of Chemistry and Life Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bState Key Laboratory of Organic Electronics and Information Displays & Institute of Advanced Materials (IAM), School of Chemistry and Life Sciences, Nanjing University of Posts & Telecommunications (NJUPT), Nanjing 210023, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors (Y. F. Li, R. Jiao and X. Y. Shen) contributed equally

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Aqueous zinc batteries have attracted wide attentions due to their high security and theoretical capacity. However, intrinsic hexagon flake stacking mechanism of Zn metal anode raises uncontrollable Zn dendrite growth and poor electrochemistry property. Herein, innovative spherical Zn metal growth mechanism is firstly proposed assisting by graphene quantum dots (GQDs) through electroplating process. The strongest adsorption energy between Zn2+ and GQDs is formed for preferential GQDs-Zn clusters producing, and then the nucleation of Zn surrounding GQDs is ideally happened. After the subsequent remainder Zn2+ plating, the homogeneous composite Zn spheres are prepared on Cu foam skeleton. Remarkably, GQDs cores fixed in the matrix always lead to the recoverable spherical Zn growth procedure during repetitive plating/stripping processes. Combining the preferential Zn (002) crystal texture, the significant spherical Zn metal anode reaches stable thermodynamics to effective suppression of Zn dendrites growth and side reactions occurrence. Under ultrahigh current density of 20 mA cm−2, the stability of 908 cycles is achieved in half-cell. This work provides a new mechanism for designing neotype Zn metal anode to thoroughly avoid the natural drawbacks of traditional hexagon shape Zn, which is significant for the practice application of aqueous zinc batteries.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Owing to the inferior security of commercial Li ion batteries originating from incidental thermal runaway in organic electrolyte, developing next-generation rechargeable batteries with enhanced security and desirable electrochemical property is highly necessary [1–6]. Aqueous zinc batteries adopting aqueous electrolytes essentially solve the risk of fire, accompanying the characteristics of cost effectiveness and high theoretical capacity (5855 mAh cm−3 and 820 mAh g−1), making them attracting widely attentions [7–12]. However, uneven Zn deposition deriving from the unstable thermodynamics tends to the uncontrollable Zn dendrite growth during repeated plating/stripping, leading to the reduced Zn metal utilization, excessive side reactions and even battery failure [13–18].

It is desirable to construct three-dimensional (3D) skeleton for Zn deposition to suppress dendrites growth via significantly reduced local current density and even electric field distribution [19–21]. For example, Duan and coworkers employed 3D printing to synthesize a 3D polymer lattices for the sequential deposition of Ni and Zn metal via electroless plating/electroplating procedures, obtaining the low Zn nucleation overpotential of approximately 40 mV [22]. Similarly, Li and coworkers reported that 3D interconnected ZnF2 matrix as protective layer and Zn deposition site was introduced in Zn metal anode, the cycling stability of symmetrical cell increases to 800 h at 1.0 mA cm−2 and 1.0 mAh cm−2 [23]. Although some improvements in stabilizing Zn metal anode have been achieved, the electrochemical performances and dendrite suppression in long cycling are still unsatisfied considering the required practical application, because the natural Zn growth mechanism of hexagon shape with diverse crystal textures tends to the dendrite formation for stable thermodynamics [24–32].

Controlling the Zn metal depositing layer with preferential crystallographic orientation is also an effective strategy for stable plating/stripping behavior [33–35]. Zn (002) crystal plane possesses the lowest surface energy and highest stacking density compared with other crystal textures of Zn metal. However, Zn (100) and Zn (101) crystal planes with significant potential to the formation of incompact Zn dendrite flakes are highly preferential formed during Zn plating. Therefore, many studies, such as introducing AgZn3 interface layer on Zn metal anode [36], constructing horizontal graphene substrate for Zn deposition [37] and adopting β-cyclodextrin as electrolyte additive [17], are proposed to regulate Zn (002) crystal plane based on lattice matching fundamental. Employing graphene quantum dots with various functional groups as electrolyte additive is another strategy to manage the deposition behavior of Zn anode [39,40]. However, the random stacking of hexagon shape Zn flakes is inevitable, especially under high current density [41–43]. Therefore, excepting conventional structure and crystal texture regulations, an innovative Zn growth pattern is highly recommended to overcome the nature drawbacks of hexagon flakes stacking.

Herein, the significantly spherical Zn metal growth mechanism is successfully proposed under the management effect of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) in electroplating process. Benefiting from the abundant negatively charged oxygen-containing functional groups of GQDs, the GQDs-Zn clusters are preferential formed, following the subsequent remainder Zn2+ plating, and the uniform Zn spheres synthesized on 3D Cu foam skeleton using GQDs as cores. Interestingly, the spherical Zn is completely maintained during repetitive plating/stripping due to the constant GQDs sites anchored in the matrix. The preferential Zn (002) crystal texture with highly stable energy is simultaneously achieved by GQDs regulating further assisting dendrite-free Cu@GQDs@Zn anode without drastic side reactions. Cu@GQDs@Zn-10 exhibits ultra-long Coulombic efficiency (CE) stability of 1861 cycles at 5 mA cm−2 and 1 mAh cm−2 with the superior average CE of 99.9%. When ultrahigh current density of 20 mA cm−2 performed, the stability of 908 cycles still maintained. The pouch full cell based MnO2 cathode drives the LED even under deformation states.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Proposition of spherical Zn metal growth mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 1 a, GQDs with the size of 3–6 nm are successfully prepared by typical electrochemical exfoliation of graphite rod. X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of GQDs exhibits obvious crystal texture of graphite (JCPDS 41–1487), and the orderly lattice spacing of 0.337 nm is indexed to the (002) crystal plane (Figure S1). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum of GQDs contains five strong peaks located at 3432.7, 1716.4, 1438.2, 1243.8 and 1047.6 cm−1, which are corresponding to hydroxyl -OH, carboxy CO, aromatic CC, epoxide/ether CO-C and CO, respectively, demonstrating the present of abundant oxygen-contain functional groups on the surface of GQDs (Fig. 1b). Furthermore, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement also definitely exhibits the abundant functional groups. The peaks at 288.9, 286.7 and 284.8 eV in C 1 s spectrum are attributed to CO, CO and CC, respectively (Fig. 1c). Meanwhile, CO and CO are also observed at 533.2 and 532.1 eV in O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 1d). Benefiting from the unique structure, GQDs show satisfactory dispersibility in electroplating aqueous solution (Fig. S2).

The motion behavior of Zn2+ induced by GQDs and Cu foam skeleton in electroplating process is predicted and analyzed by density functional theory (DFT) calculations. Initially, optimized carbon cluster structure containing carboxyl, hydroxy and ether functional groups corresponds to GQDs (Figure S3). One ionic Zn is separately absorbed by three kinds of functional groups, and the corresponding adsorption energies (Ead) are −2.07, −1.89 and −1.68 eV, respectively (Fig. 1e) [44]. Then, the adsorption behavior between Zn2+ and Cu metal (corresponding to Cu foam) is studied (Fig. 1f and Fig. S4). Two kinds of adsorption stabilization sites are presented on the surface of Cu (111), the adsorption energies of ionic Zn in hollow and top sites are −0.95 and −0.70 eV, respectively. Compared with GQDs, the relatively low adsorption energy between Zn2+ and Cu foam demonstrates the weaker interaction. Speculatively, ionic Zn is preferentially adsorbed around GQDs by strong adsorption forming uniform GQDs-Zn clusters in electroplating solution (Fig. 1g). Then GQDs as active centers for Zn nucleation and growth anchor on the surface of Cu foam under the electro field driving, leading to a spherical Zn metal growth mechanism (correspond to Cu@GQDs@Zn). When the GQDs regulation disappearing, the intrinsic Zn morphology of hexagon flake is recovered on Cu foam (correspond to Cu@Zn, Fig. 1h).

As expected, after electroplating completed under the presence of 10 mg GQDs (Cu@GQDs@Zn-10), the originally smooth surface of Cu foam is completely encapsulated by dense Zn metal layer with the thickness of approximately 16.8 μm (Figure S5, S6 and Fig. 2 a). Furthermore, the spherical Zn with diameter of 1–3 μm is also clearly observed after amplification, demonstrating the neotype growth mechanism of spherical Zn metal is successfully achieved (Fig. 2b). Field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) elemental mapping impressively exhibits the uniform distribution of C, O and Zn elements in Cu@GQDs@Zn-10, in which the C and O elements are originated from GQDs (Fig. 2d-g). The week Cu element sign further demonstrates the structure of Zn-encapsulated Cu foam in Cu@GQDs@Zn-10 (Fig. 2f). Obviously, the intrinsic growth mechanism, namely, stacking of hexagon shape Zn flakes is thoroughly changed via the introduction of heterogeneous nucleation/growth center of GQDs for Zn metal.

In order to deeply explored the neotype mechanism of spherical Zn metal, the sample is prepared by electroplating performed on copper grid for transmission electron microscopy (TEM) test. It is clear that aggregated Zn growth is unambiguously happened around GQDs centers for composite sphere structure, in accordance well with the predicted spherical Zn growth mechanism (Fig. S7 and Fig. 2h). Furthermore, the forming process of spherical Zn in Cu@GQDs@Zn-10 is observed in detail by SEM measurement (Fig. S8). Firstly, the rough surface composed of stacked Zn granules is formed under the guidance of GQDs in initial Zn electroplating at the electroplating time of 20 min (Fig. S8a, b). Then, the interspace between Zn granules is filled by subsequent Zn deposition, leading to the flatter surface at the electroplating time of 40 min (Fig. S8c, d). As electroplating continues, the GQDs-Zn clusters in electroplating solution still keep depositing on Cu foam. The distinguishable spherical Zn is certainly obtained on the surface of Cu@GQDs@Zn-10 attributing to without the following Zn fill process (Fig. S8e, f). Undoubtedly, the intrinsic morphology of hexagon shape Zn flakes is recovered without the addition of GQDs in electroplating solution (Cu@Zn), demonstrating the management effect of GQDs enables spherical Zn morphology realized for strong practical application of aqueous zinc batteries with neotype electrochemical mechanism of Zn metal anode (Fig. S9).

GQDs as nucleation/growth centers for Zn metal highly determines the structure of Zn sphere. When the GQDs content decreases to 5 mg (Cu@GQDs@Zn-5), even the tendency of spherical Zn appears, the deposited Zn metal still in hexagon shape flakes due to scanty GQDs sites, resulting in too week regulation for spherical Zn compared with that in Cu@GQDs@Zn-10 (Fig. S10a, b). Conversely, as the GQDs content increases to 15 mg (Cu@GQDs@Zn-15), a lot of irregular Zn particles are generated on the surface of Cu foam. This is because the excess GQDs cores for Zn growth leads to the subsequent Zn fill process disappearing (Fig. S10c, d).

It is worth mentioning that most Zn metal shows the lattice spacing of 0.247 nm around GQDs centers, which is attributed to Zn (002) crystal plane accompanying optimal restraint effect for dendrite growth and by-reactions occurred (Fig. 2h, i). The preferential Zn (002) crystal texture is predictively also achieved by GQDs centers induced effect. The peaks in the XRD curves of Cu@Zn and Cu@GQDs@Zn-5/10/15 are just indexed to pure Cu (JCPDS 04–0836) and Zn (JCPDS 04–0831; Fig. 2j). Thereinto, the peaks located at 36.3° and 39.0° are corresponding to Zn (002) and (100) lattice planes, respectively. The intensity ratio of one (I002/I100) is increased from 0.43 to 0.90, 1.15 and 1.18 as the content of GQDs increases from 0 to 5, 10 and 15 mg, respectively, indicating the intensity of Zn (002) lattice plane is gradually increased as the enhanced regulation of GQDs. For fundamentally understanding the phenomenon of the GQDs in promoting preferred crystal orientation, DFT calculation for absorbed behaviors on Zn (002) and (100) crystal planes are produced. The absorption energies for GQDs cluster structure on Zn (002) and (100) are calculated to be −2.00 eV, −1.76 eV, respectively, revealing its preferential interaction with (002) crystal plane (Fig. 2k, l and Figure S11, S12).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sustainability of spherical Zn structure in cycling
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology evolution during Zn stripping/plating demonstrates that Cu@GQDs@Zn-10 possesses unique working mechanism compared with that of Cu@Zn and Zn foil. When 5 mAh cm−2 of Zn striped from Cu@GQDs@Zn-10, the spherical Zn is totally disappeared and concomitant flat surface is emerged without flaky Zn dendrite generated (Fig. 3 a, d, e). More impressively, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of electrolyte after Zn stripping perfectly coincides with that of pristine one without the absorption peak at 304 nm generated from GQDs dispersed in electrolyte, indicating the GQDs in Cu@GQDs@Zn-10 are not shed during Zn stripping (Fig. S13). In subsequent 5 mAh cm−2 of Zn plating, the GQDs fixed in original positions of Cu@GQDs@Zn-10 guide Zn nucleation/deposition behavior by GQDs-Zn clusters near skeleton, resulting in the uniform spherical structure similar to initial Cu@GQDs@Zn-10 recovered (Fig. 3f). The spherical Zn growth mechanism is sustainable due to GQDs are always caged in matrix of Cu@GQDs@Zn-10 in repeated plating/stripping, generating the perpetual management effect. In addition, CO and CO are still observed in C 1 s and O 1 s XPS spectra of Cu@GQDs@Zn-10 after 50 cycles in CE cycling, indicating the superior stability of oxygen-containing functional groups on GQDs (Fig. S14). On the contrary, the disorderly flaky Zn dendrites are remarkably presented on the surface of Cu@Zn and Zn foil after just a cycle (Fig. 3b, c and g-l). Undoubtedly, spherical Zn growth mechanism possesses significant advanced nature compared with intrinsic Zn flakes.

Benefiting from the unique morphology and Zn growth mechanism, the more even Zn plating is directly observed in open cell by in situ electron microscopy measurement. Cu@GQDs@Zn-10 maintains the initial morphology in cross-sectional view even Zn deposition for 60 min at the current of 5 and 10 mA cm−2, attributing to the uniform Zn layer is achieved on the surface of Cu@GQDs@Zn-10 (Fig. S15a and S16a). However, the distinct Zn dendrite growth inevitably occurs on the surface of Cu@Zn and Zn foil after Zn deposition only for 30 min due to aggregative Zn distribution resulting from the inferior skeleton structure (Fig. S15b, c and S16b, c). After that, the evident bubbles appear on the both surfaces of Cu@Zn and Zn foil (Fig. S17b, c). Inversely, almost no bubbles are observed on the surface of Cu@GQDs@Zn-10, indicating the hydrogen evolution reaction is effectively prevented (Fig. S17a). Moreover, the by-reactions between Cu@GQDs@Zn-10 anode and electrolyte are effectively restrained. The surface of Cu@GQDs@Zn-10 is still flat without dramatic protrusions of Zn dendrites after complete immersion for a week, and meanwhile, the XRD pattern of Cu@GQDs@Zn-10 just exhibits the peaks for Zn and Cu (Fig. S15d and g). Inversely, obvious Zn dendrites and by-reaction of Zn4SO4(OH)6•5H2O (JCPDS 39–0688) are observed in SEM images and XRD curves for Cu@Zn and Zn foil, respectively, demonstrating the presence of tempestuous by-reactions due to absent regulation effect of GQDs (Figure S15e, f, h, i).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ultrafast plating/stripping kinetics of spherical Zn
ce:section.ce:para: Chronoamperometry (CA) measurement was employed to further identify the Zn nucleation mechanism on various Zn anodes (Fig. 4 a). In initial stage, the values of current density suffer from dramatic decrease as the time increases, suggesting a 2D diffusion process which tends to induce rough Zn growth, while the uniform Zn deposition is achieved until the values of current density steadied. Owing to the presence of GQDs, Cu@GQDs@Zn-10 quickly enters the homogeneous Zn nucleation/growth period just after the transitory 2D diffusion of ∼48 s, which is much shorter than that of Cu@Zn (∼124 s) and Zn foil (∼185 s).

In order to evaluate the electrochemical plating/stripping reversibility, Cu@GQDs@Zn-10//Cu, Cu@Zn//Cu and Zn foil//Cu half-cell are assembled. After the activization process, CE stability of Cu@GQDs@Zn-10//Cu significantly enhances due to the regulation effect of GQDs and restorable growth mechanism of spherical Zn in Cu@GQDs@Zn-10 electrode. Specifically, Cu@GQDs@Zn-10//Cu delivers an ultra-long CE stability of 1861 cycles at 5 mA cm−2 and 1 mAh cm−2 with the superior average CE of 99.9%, while that of Cu@Zn//Cu and Zn foil//Cu suffers from dramatic fluctuation after about 1300 and 30 cycles, respectively (Fig. 4d). As the ultrahigh current density of 20 mA cm−2 performed, Cu@GQDs@Zn-10//Cu still maintains the stable CE of 908 cycles with the average CE of 99.3%, which is much higher than that of Cu@Zn//Cu (420 cycles), Zn foil//Cu (380 cycles), Cu@GQDs@Zn-5//Cu (600 cycles) and Cu@GQDs@Zn-15//Cu (450 cycles, Fig. 4e and S18). Worth mentioning that the CE performance of Cu@GQDs@Zn-10 is fairly competitive among the reported composite Zn metal anodes for aqueous zinc batteries (Table S1 and Fig. 4c). Simultaneously, the polarization of Cu@GQDs@Zn-10//Cu is stable at ∼0.14 V from 1st to 100th cycles at high current density of 20 mA cm−2, while Zn foil//Cu and Cu@Zn//Cu show insecure high polarization at ∼0.43 V and ∼0.21 V, respectively (Fig. 4b and S19). The inferior CE performances of Zn foil//Cu and Cu@Zn//Cu are mainly attributed to the rampant growth of Zn dendrites on both electrodes (Fig. 4f, g, i, j). As expected, Cu@GQDs@Zn-10//Cu still maintains flat and dense surfaces after 200 cycles (Fig. 4h, k).

Except the superior CE stability, Cu@GQDs@Zn-10 also possesses satisfactory cycle and rate performance in symmetrical cell (Fig. 5 ). First, Cu@GQDs@Zn-10 possesses stable average overpotentials of 18, 19, 22, 30, 42, 46 mV at the current densities of 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 10 mA cm−2 with the fixed capacity of 1 mAh cm−2, which is distinctly batter than that of Cu@Zn (31, 35, 41, 54, 68 and 76 mV) and Zn foil (39, 45, 75, 99, 140 and 155 mV, Fig. 5c). Cu@GQDs@Zn-10 exhibits stable cycle performance up to about 1200 h at 1 mA cm−2 and 1 mAh cm−2 with the lower overpotentials of 24 and 26 mV at the periods of 205–210 h and 995–1000 h, respectively (Fig. 5a). However, the corresponding overpotentials are increased to 28 and 36 mV for Cu@Zn, which even reaches to ∼80 mV at 1200 h. Worse, Zn foil accompanying an overpotential of ∼50 mV is short-circuited before 200 h cycling. When the current density increases to 10 mA cm−2, Cu@GQDs@Zn-10 still has stable cycle of 500 h (2500 cycles) and the overpotentials are stabilized at approximately 35 and 39 mV at 100th and 400th h, respectively (Fig. 5b). Obviously, the overpotential of Cu@Zn is always significantly higher than that of Cu@GQDs@Zn-10, as well as Zn foil has been in unstable state until failure at ∼260 h.

The distinguishing cycle performance between Cu@GQDs@Zn-10, Cu@Zn and Zn foil is further studied by SEM measurement. After 200 h cycling at the current density of 10 mA cm−2, the surfaces of Cu@Zn and Zn foil become visually uneven (Fig. 5d, g). The strong peaks for by-products of Zn4SO4(OH)6•3H2O (JCPDS 39–0689) and Zn4SO4(OH)6•5H2O are observed in the XRD curves of Cu@Zn and Zn foil, respectively, indicating the intense by-reactions happened (Fig. S20a, b). Furthermore, the promiscuous and massive Zn dendrites are generated on the surface of Cu@Zn and Zn foil, resulting in gradually deteriorative cycling stability (Fig. 5e, f, h, i). Inversely, the corresponding negative phenomena are all prevented on Cu@GQDs@Zn-10 electrode, and the pure phase and uniform morphology are invariable maintained (Fig. 5j-l, S20c). In addition, the kinetic processes of three symmetrical cells are researched by EIS test before and after 50 cycles (Fig. 5m, n, o). The charge interfacial resistance, corresponding to high-frequency semicircle, of Cu@GQDs@Zn-10 is reduced from ∼10 Ω in fresh state to ∼2 Ω after 50 cycles, which is much smaller than that of Cu@Zn (form ∼24 Ω to ∼8 Ω) and Zn foil (form ∼1340 Ω to ∼960 Ω), indicating the superior Zn deposition/stripping kinetics and stable interface of Cu@GQDs@Zn-10.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical tests of aqueous zinc batteries
ce:section.ce:para: In order to assemble the zinc full batteries in the aqueous electrolyte (2 M ZnSO4 and 0.2 M MnSO4), MnO2 as cathode material was prepared by typical hydrothermal method [51]. SEM image shows uniform MnO2 nanowires, as well as XRD pattern demonstrates the presence of pure α-MnO2 (Fig. S21a, b). Cu@GQDs@Zn-10//MnO2, Cu@Zn//MnO2 and Zn foil//MnO2 full cells deliver similar redox peaks at the same positions of voltage, illustrating Cu@GQDs@Zn-10 and Cu@Zn anodes can take part in electrochemical reaction as commercial Zn foil in zinc batteries (Fig. 6 a). Furthermore, Cu@GQDs@Zn-10//MnO2 exhibits a high initial discharge capacity of ∼238 mAh g−1 at the current density of 0.5 A g−1 with a satisfied capacity retention ratio of 76% after 340 cycles (Fig. 6d). However, Cu@Zn//MnO2 and Zn foil//MnO2 are all suffered from rapid capacity fading from ∼186 and ∼182 mAh g−1 to ∼119 and ∼78 mAh g−1 just after 50 cycles with the inferior retention ratio of 61% and 29%, respectively. Moreover, Cu@GQDs@Zn-10//MnO2 possesses minimal charge interfacial resistance (∼3 Ω) and polarizing voltage (25 mV) compared with that of Cu@Zn//MnO2 (∼6 Ω, 30 mV) and Zn foil//MnO2 (∼1031 Ω, 31 mV), which highly stems from the reduced current density and enhanced charge-transfer behavior on Cu@GQDs@Zn-10/electrolyte interface (Fig. 6b, c and S22).

Cu@GQDs@Zn-10//MnO2 also has desirable rate capacities of 254, 253, 214, 169 and 130 mAh g−1 at the current density of 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1.0 and 2.0 A g−1, which is much higher than that of Cu@Zn//MnO2 (243, 247, 196, 154 and 113 mAh g−1) and Zn foil//MnO2 (240, 209, 150, 106 and 76 mAh g−1), indicating the faster kinetic process happened in Cu@GQDs@Zn-10//MnO2 (Fig. 6e). The pouch full cell of Cu@GQDs@Zn-10//MnO2 is packaged to lighten LEDs for further practical application of Cu@GQDs@Zn-10 anode (Fig. 6f). Interestingly, the pouch full cell till has stable energy output even under various deformation states.

In summary, a neotype spherical Zn growth mechanism assisted by the targeted regulation of GQDs is successfully proposed as a promising substitution for intrinsic hexagon Zn flakes stacking pattern to enhance the effectiveness and security of aqueous zinc batteries. As DFT calculation predicting, GQDs-Zn clusters are preferential formed through stronger interaction in electroplating process due to abundant oxygen-containing functional groups of GQDs. After further evolution, the spherical Zn using GQDs as cores are uniformly produced on the surface of Cu foam skeleton. Interestingly, the spherical Zn structure is highly reversible during cycling due to the fixed GQDs sites in matrix, realizing ultrafast plating/stripping kinetics. Combining preferential crystallographic orientation of Zn (002), the detrimental Zn dendrites, side reactions and hydrogen evolution reaction are effectively restrained on the interface between spherical Zn metal anode and aqueous electrolyte. Therefore, the neotype Cu@GQDs@Zn-10 electrode exhibits ultra-long CE stability of 1861 cycles at 5 mA cm−2 and 1 mAh cm−2 with the superior average CE of 99.9%. Even at the ultrahigh current density of 20 mA cm−2, the stability of 908 cycles still maintained. A serious full cells matching with MnO2 cathode demonstrates the considerable practical application of spherical Zn metal anode in aqueous zinc batteries.

Yan-Fei Li: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Rui Jiao: Data curation, Formal analysis. Xiao-Yan Shen: Data curation, Formal analysis. Yi-Han Song: Investigation. Lei Ding: Methodology. Guo-Duo Yang: Resources. Changlu Shao: Supervision. Xing-Long Wu: Supervision. Jing-Ping Zhang: Supervision. Ming-Xiao Deng: Supervision. Shao-Lei Wang: Supervision. Hai-Zhu Sun: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22275030 and 22209023), the Fundamental Research Funds (Science and Technology Achievements Transformation) for the Central Universities of China (CGZH202203), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22035001), and Jilin Provincial Education Department.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.ensm.2023.102934.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Experimental Section; electrochemical measurement; DFT calculation; Figures S1-S22: XRD pattern, photograph, optimized cluster structure, SEM images, UV–vis absorption spectra, XPS curves, in situ electron microscopy, CE stability, plating and stripping voltage profiles, EIS plots; Table S1: comparison the CE stability of Cu@GQDs@Zn-10 with references.
